---
title: "Oil Price Shocks, Exchange Rate, and Inflation in Kazakhstan"
subtitle: "ECON 336 - Programming for Economics"
author: "Bono Almatov, Madiyar Serik, Mukhamediyar Nurzhanov, Nurkhan Utibayev"
date: "`r format(Sys.Date(), '%B %d, %Y')`"
output:
  pdf_document:
    latex_engine: pdflatex
    number_sections: true
    toc: false
fontsize: 11pt
geometry: margin=1in
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE, fig.align = "center")

setup_candidates <- c(
  "../Code_database/00_setup.R",
  "Code_database/00_setup.R",
  "00_setup.R"
)
source(setup_candidates[file.exists(setup_candidates)][1])

if (!file.exists(path_here("Output", "table3_regression_results.csv"))) {
  source(path_here("Code_estimation", "02_estimate_results.R"))
}
if (!file.exists(path_here("Output", "table4_robustness_checks.csv"))) {
  source(path_here("Code_appendix", "03_robustness_checks.R"))
}

analysis_data <- read_csv(path_here("Derived", "monthly_analysis_data.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  mutate(date = as.Date(date))
desc <- read_csv(path_here("Output", "table1_descriptive_stats.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
first_stage <- read_csv(path_here("Output", "table2_first_stage_summary.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
reg_results <- read_csv(path_here("Output", "table3_regression_results.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
robustness <- read_csv(path_here("Output", "table4_robustness_checks.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)

fmt <- function(x, digits = 3) formatC(x, format = "f", digits = digits)
fmt_p <- function(x) ifelse(x < 0.001, "<0.001", fmt(x, 3))

sample_start <- min(analysis_data$date[!is.na(analysis_data$lag_inflation) & analysis_data$date >= as.Date("2000-03-01")])
est_dates <- analysis_data %>%
  filter(date >= as.Date("2000-03-01"), date <= as.Date("2019-01-01"), !is.na(lag_inflation))
sample_end <- max(est_dates$date)
n_obs <- reg_results$n[reg_results$model == "IV: Brent instrument"]

fs_beta <- first_stage$estimate[1]
fs_f <- first_stage$first_stage_f[1]
iv_beta <- reg_results$estimate[reg_results$model == "IV: Brent instrument"]
iv_se <- reg_results$std_error[reg_results$model == "IV: Brent instrument"]
iv_p <- reg_results$p_value[reg_results$model == "IV: Brent instrument"]
iv_ten <- reg_results$ten_percent_effect[reg_results$model == "IV: Brent instrument"]
iv_ten_low <- reg_results$ten_percent_low[reg_results$model == "IV: Brent instrument"]
iv_ten_high <- reg_results$ten_percent_high[reg_results$model == "IV: Brent instrument"]
ols_beta <- reg_results$estimate[reg_results$model == "OLS"]
```

# Abstract

Our project asks if a depreciation of the Kazakhstan's currency - tenge - increases inflation of consumer price when the depreciation is predicted by changes in global Brent crude oil prices. We build a monthly dataset for Kazakhstan by combining Brent prices, the USD/KZT exchange rate, consumer price inflation (CPI), and some annual macroeconomic context variables. We committed OLS through 2 stages: at first, we used monthly log changes in Brent oil prices for prediction of monthly log changes in USD/KZT; and the second stage is estimation of the effect of predicted KZT depreciation on monthly CPI inflation. We used samplesize of `r n_obs` monthly observations from `r format(sample_start, '%B %Y')` till `r format(sample_end, '%B %Y')`. The first stage is negative ( It is expected to be so, since Kazakhstan is an oil-exporting economy): a 10 percent increase in crude oil prices predicts approximately `r fmt(10 * fs_beta)` percentage points (p.p.) less tenge depreciation. However our preferred IV estimate does not show that Brent-predicted depreciation increases inflation. A depreciation of 10 percent causes a `r fmt(iv_ten)` p.p. change in monthly inflation, with a 95% confidence interval (CI) from `r fmt(iv_ten_low)` to `r fmt(iv_ten_high)`. So, even though OLS results show a positive relation, an IV result lacks statistical significance and has the minus sign, suggesting either weak pass-through in this design or violation of the exclusion restriction because change in oil prices can affect Kazakhstan's economy in many different ways like through fiscal revenue, domestic energy costs, and broader macroeconomic expectations.

# Research Question and Motivation

Our chosen country, Kazakhstan, is a good case to study how exchange-rate passes through because the economy here is depended on oil prices and the currency, tenge, has
gone through some large exchange-rate movements during the sample period. In the raw data: Brent prices, USD/KZT, and inflation go together around major stress 
episodes, like 2008-2009 and 2014-2016. These episodes make the question economically important because when a depreciation happens to currency, households immediately 
think about whether the depreciation quickly appears in consumer prices. Also policymakers care about whether inflation rate is mostly imported through the exchange 
rate or driven by domestic demand.

Our research question is:

> Does depreciation of the Kazakhstani tenge, predicted by changes in global Brent crude oil prices, increase consumer price inflation?

The phrase "predicted by Brent crude oil prices" is very important in this context. The simple regression of inflation on the exchange rate can be biased because the exchange rate responds to the same things that influence prices, such as inflation expectations, policy changes, and macroeconomic shocks. Therefore, we use in our research Brent oil price changes as an instrument for tenge depreciation. As we know, Kazakhstan is an oil producer, so negative oil price shocks should weaken export revenue. Also it needs to reduce foreign-exchange inflows and put downward pressure on the tenge. We used the instrumented estimate to isolate the part of depreciation which comes from external oil-market shocks rather than from domestic inflation itself.

This project connects to the exchange-rate pass-through literature, which studies how exchange-rate movements affect import prices and consumer prices. This literature gives a common result that pass-through is incomplete and varies by other factors, like country, monetary-policy credibility, inflation history, and the imported share of consumption. The topic is especially relevant for Kazakhstan because oil prices can move the exchange rate even when domestic consumer demand has not changed. That gives the project a clear empirical source of variation. But it also creates a challenge, which is that oil prices could influence inflation through other means besides the exchange rate.

# Data

For this project we collected data from the reliable sources and put it in project's file as raw files for the replication package. The data for daily Brent crude oil prices was from the FRED file `fred_dcoilbrenteu.csv`; daily USD/KZT exchange rates were gathered from the National Bank of Kazakhstan file `nationalbank_usd_kzt_exchange_rate.xlsx`; and monthly CPI data is from `kazakhstan_indexcpi_statbureau.csv`, where each month is reported as an index and where the previous month is equal to 100. The World Bank macro file is used to give an annual context and to complete a single robustness check. Additional CPI sources are in `Raw_data` and are applicable for documentation but are not needed for the baseline monthly estimations.

However we have one key limitation that is the monthly CPI file ending in January 2019. Despite tha Brent and exchange-rate files continue beyond 2019, the estimation must stop with the last monthly inflation outcome. Therefore, the final baseline sample runs from March 2000 through January 2019 because inflation is included as a control.

Our code for cleaning makes three main transformations. At first, it averages daily Brent prices to the month and converts into monthly log changes:

\[
\Delta oil_t = 100 \times \left[\log(Brent_t) - \log(Brent_{t-1})\right].
\]

Secondly, daily exchange rates are averaged to the month too and changes to tenge depreciation:

\[
\Delta e_t = 100 \times \left[\log(USD/KZT_t) - \log(USD/KZT_{t-1})\right].
\]

Since the exchange rate we have is KZT per USD, a positive value in our calculation means the tenge is depreciated. Third, CPI inflation is constructed from the monthly CPI indexes:

\[
\pi_t = 100 \times \log(CPIIndex_t / 100).
\]

This makes inflation, depreciation, and oil price changes comparable as monthly log percentage changes.

```{r descriptive-table}
desc_print <- desc %>%
  mutate(
    variable = dplyr::recode(
      variable,
      inflation_mom = "Monthly CPI inflation",
      dep_kzt = "Tenge depreciation",
      oil_log_change = "Brent log change",
      brent_price = "Brent price",
      usd_kzt = "USD/KZT"
    ),
    across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 3))
  )

knitr::kable(
  desc_print,
  caption = "Descriptive statistics for the estimation sample",
  booktabs = TRUE
)
```

```{r fig1, out.width='94%', fig.cap='Monthly inflation, exchange rate, and Brent oil prices'}
knitr::include_graphics(path_here("Output", "figure1_time_series.png"))
```

Figure 1 above shows the basic time-series patterns: the exchange rate is relatively stable during early years and then rises sharply with depreciation periods; inflation is usually low month to month with sudden spikes around the large exchange-rate movements; brent prices are volatile, especially during the global financial crisis and the 2014-2016 oil-price decline. These patterns motivate the instrumental-variables design: oil price shocks are shown in the exchange-rate graph and provide external variation in tenge depreciation.

# Econometric Model

The baseline model is a monthly two-stage least squares specification. The first stage is:

\[
\Delta e_t = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 \Delta oil_t + \rho \pi_{t-1} + \lambda_m + v_t,
\]

where \(\Delta e_t\) is monthly tenge depreciation, \(\Delta oil_t\) is the monthly Brent log change, \(\pi_{t-1}\) is lagged monthly inflation, and \(\lambda_m\) are month fixed effects. The month fixed effects absorb normal seasonality in prices and exchange-rate patterns.

The second stage is:

\[
\pi_t = \beta \widehat{\Delta e_t} + \gamma \pi_{t-1} + \lambda_m + u_t.
\]

The parameter we are interested about is \(\beta\). It tells us the percentage-point change in monthly CPI inflation from a 1 percent depreciation of the tenge, using only the depreciation component that's predicted by Brent oil price shocks. The effect of a 10 percent predicted depreciation is just \(10\beta\).

For this to work, we need three assumptions to hold:

1. **Relevance.** Brent price changes have to predict tenge depreciation. This is should be true because oil is a major export revenue source for Kazakhstan, and lower oil prices tend to weaken the currency.
2. **Exclusion.** Brent price changes should affect monthly CPI inflation only through the exchange rate after adjusting for lagged inflation and month fixed effects. This assumption is complicated for several reasons. Oil prices could also affect domestic fuel prices, government revenue, expectations, or demand. Because of this, the estimate should not be interpreted purely mechanical causal fact but rather as an IV pass-through estimate under a maintained exclusion assumption.
3. **Timing and exogeneity.** Global Brent prices are not influenced by Kazakhstan's monthly CPI inflation. This is because Kazakhstan is small relative to the global oil market.

In order to address serial correlation and heteroskedasticity that are typical of monthly macro data, all standard errors are Newey-West with four flags.

# Results

```{r fig2, out.width='88%', fig.cap='First-stage relationship between Brent changes and tenge depreciation'}
knitr::include_graphics(path_here("Output", "figure2_first_stage.png"))
```

The first-stage coefficient on Brent price changes is `r fmt(fs_beta)`. This means that a 10 percent increase in Brent prices predicts `r fmt(10 * fs_beta)` percentage points of tenge depreciation. Since the coefficient is negative, the interpretation is appreciation pressure when oil prices increase and depreciation pressure when oil prices fall. The robust first-stage F statistic is `r fmt(fs_f)`. This shows statistically visible relevance, but it is below the common rule-of-thumb value of 10, so the IV estimates should be interpreted with weak-instrument caution.

```{r first-stage-table}
fs_print <- first_stage %>%
  transmute(
    coefficient = "Brent oil log change",
    estimate = round(estimate, 3),
    std_error = round(std_error, 3),
    p_value = fmt_p(p_value),
    first_stage_f = round(first_stage_f, 2),
    n = n
  )

knitr::kable(
  fs_print,
  caption = "First-stage estimate: Brent price changes predicting tenge depreciation",
  booktabs = TRUE
)
```

The main second-stage results are in Table 3. The OLS estimate is included as a comparison, but the preferred specification is the IV model because it uses Brent-predicted depreciation.

```{r regression-table}
reg_print <- reg_results %>%
  transmute(
    model,
    estimate = round(estimate, 3),
    std_error = round(std_error, 3),
    p_value = fmt_p(p_value),
    ci_95 = paste0("[", round(conf_low, 3), ", ", round(conf_high, 3), "]"),
    effect_of_10pct_depreciation = round(ten_percent_effect, 3),
    n
  )

knitr::kable(
  reg_print,
  caption = "Effect of tenge depreciation on monthly CPI inflation",
  booktabs = TRUE
)
```

```{r fig3, out.width='86%', fig.cap='OLS and IV estimates of exchange-rate pass-through'}
knitr::include_graphics(path_here("Output", "figure3_pass_through_estimates.png"))
```

The IV coefficient is `r fmt(iv_beta)` with a Newey-West standard error of `r fmt(iv_se)` and a p-value of `r fmt_p(iv_p)`. The point estimate implies that a 1 percent Brent-predicted depreciation is associated with a `r fmt(iv_beta)` percentage-point change in monthly CPI inflation. For a 10 percent predicted depreciation, the implied change is `r fmt(iv_ten)` percentage points. The confidence interval is wide, from `r fmt(iv_ten_low)` to `r fmt(iv_ten_high)`, and includes zero. Therefore the preferred IV specification does not provide evidence that Brent-predicted depreciation increases monthly CPI inflation.

The OLS estimate is `r fmt(ols_beta)`, which is positive and statistically significant. This means that ordinary tenge depreciation is associated with higher monthly inflation in the raw conditional relationship. The IV result changes that conclusion. Once the exchange-rate variation is limited to the part predicted by Brent oil price changes, the estimate becomes negative and statistically insignificant. A plausible interpretation is that oil price declines both weaken the tenge and reduce some price pressures directly. If that direct oil-price channel is present, the exclusion restriction is not fully satisfied, and the IV coefficient cannot be interpreted as pure exchange-rate pass-through.

# Robustness and Interpretation

```{r robustness-table}
rob_print <- robustness %>%
  transmute(
    specification,
    outcome,
    estimate = round(estimate, 3),
    std_error = round(std_error, 3),
    p_value = fmt_p(p_value),
    ci_95 = paste0("[", round(conf_low, 3), ", ", round(conf_high, 3), "]"),
    effect_of_10pct_depreciation = round(ten_percent_effect, 3),
    n
  )

knitr::kable(
  rob_print,
  caption = "Robustness checks for the IV pass-through estimate",
  booktabs = TRUE
)
```

```{r fig4, out.width='88%', fig.cap='Robustness of the IV pass-through estimate'}
knitr::include_graphics(path_here("Output", "figure4_robustness.png"))
```

The robustness checks illustrate how much sensitivity there is to imposing further assumptions. Incorporating a linear trend examines whether the initial finding reflects largely the disinflationary effect. Incorporating the annual control variables investigates whether GDP growth, money supply, and oil rents absorb part of the oil-driven exchange-rate channel. Excluding August-to-December period investigates whether the results arise exclusively due to the significant exchange rate movements during the switch to the floating exchange rate regime. The three-month cumulative response examines whether pass-through takes place within a short time period.

Regardless of which test is performed, the point estimates continue to be negative and imprecise. This confirms the overall conclusion of the project. The project finds that there is a positive OLS relationship between tenge depreciation and inflation, but it does not find robust IV evidence that the part of depreciation driven by Brent shocks raises consumer price inflation. This is expected given that it is a monthly macroeconomic IV specification for a small economy. The underlying identifying variation is real, although it is modest and occurs in just a few instances.


# Limitations

One of the main limitation is the exclusion restriction. Oil prices can affect Kazakhstan's CPI not only through exchange rate. For example, oil prices can affect government spending, income expectations or subsidies, which in turn can affect CPI.

Sample size is another concern. The monthly CPI data ends on January 2019, so the analysis misses several shocks. We relied on monthly CPI observations available in the provided raw file, but a larger data of CPI would allow a stronger analysis.

Third limitation is aggregation of data. Our sample captures only monthly averages, which are useful for matching daily financial data to monthly inflation, but they miss daily variation, so in that sense we can observe only marco-level movement, instead of desired micro-level one. If the exchange rate changed at the end of a month, CPI would lag behing and respond only next month. The three-month robustness check partially addresses this issue, but a richer model might show detailed changes and analysis.

# Conclusion

The evidence does not show that Brent-predicted tenge depreciation affects consumer price inflation in Kazakhstan. First stage behaves as expected for an oil-exporting economy: Brent price increases are associated with less tenge depreciation, while oil price decreases are associated with depreciation. But using IV model, a 10 percent Brent-predicted depreciation is associated with a `r fmt(iv_ten)` percentage-point change in monthly CPI inflation and the estimate is statistically insignificant.

The result is still informative. It shows that exchange-rate movements and inflation are positively correlated in OLS, but the oil-price would not suit as an instrument variable. This finding illustrates that oil shocks can affect both the exchange rate and inflation, especially in oil-dependent economies such as Kazakhstan. To improve the research, it would be helpful to extend the monthly CPI data beyond 2019 and estimate its effect on food, non-food goods and services, where the role of oil-price and import exposure may differ.

# Replication Package

The replication folder is structured as follows. `Raw_data` contains the original data files. `Code_database/01_build_monthly_data.R` creates the cleaned monthly dataset in `Derived`. `Code_estimation/02_estimate_results.R` creates the descriptive table, first-stage table, main regression table, and Figures 1-3. `Code_appendix/03_robustness_checks.R` creates the robustness table and Figure 4. `run_all.R` runs the whole workflow and renders this paper.

# References

Burstein, Ariel, and Gita Gopinath. 2014. "International Prices and Exchange Rates." In *Handbook of International Economics*, Vol. 4.

Campa, Jose Manuel, and Linda S. Goldberg. 2005. "Exchange Rate Pass-Through into Import Prices." *Review of Economics and Statistics* 87(4): 679-690.

Bureau of National Statistics of Kazakhstan. Consumer price index data files used in this replication package.

Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis. FRED daily Brent crude oil price file used in this replication package.

National Bank of Kazakhstan. USD/KZT exchange-rate file used in this replication package.

World Bank. Kazakhstan macroeconomic indicators file used in this replication package.
